title: Microsoft GraphRAG: Knowledge-Graph-Based RAG with Community Summaries
one_liner: Uses Microsoft's graphrag library to build an LLM-extracted knowledge graph with Leiden community summaries at index time, enabling local (entity-centric) and global (whole-corpus sensemaking) search that flat top-k chunk retrieval cannot answer.
problem_solved: Standard chunk-embed-retrieve RAG fails on questions whose answer is not contained in any single chunk: holistic 'summarize the whole corpus' / 'what are the major themes' questions, and questions requiring connections between facts scattered across disparate documents. GraphRAG pre-computes an entity-relationship graph and hierarchical community summaries so the LLM can reason over corpus-wide structure instead of a handful of similar chunks.
failure_modes: Questions about the corpus as a whole (themes, major accomplishments, overall trends) return shallow or wrong answers because no single retrieved chunk contains the global picture
Answers that require connecting related information spread across many documents or sections (multi-hop / disparate-source synthesis) miss key links
Entity-centric questions (all companies founded by X, everything related to entity Y) return incomplete answers because relevant facts are scattered beyond the top-k similar chunks
Similarity search retrieves chunks that mention the query terms but the system never aggregates or counts across the corpus
Complex semantic concepts are misunderstood because retrieval sees isolated text fragments without the relationships between entities
when_to_use: Your users ask global sensemaking questions over a corpus ('what are the main themes/risks/accomplishments in these documents') rather than needle-in-haystack lookups
Answers require synthesizing facts about the same entities from many different places in the corpus (multi-hop, aggregation, 'how are X and Y connected')
The corpus is relatively static and re-read often, so a one-time expensive indexing pass amortizes well
You want an off-the-shelf, batteries-included graph RAG pipeline (CLI-driven) rather than hand-rolling entity extraction and graph traversal
Entity-rich text (biographies, news, intelligence reports, organizational documents) where entities and relationships are the natural backbone of answers
tradeoffs: Indexing is dramatically more expensive and slower than embedding-only indexing: many LLM calls (entity/relationship extraction, community summarization) over every chunk; the notebook explicitly warns about this cost
Query-time global search runs an LLM map-reduce over community summaries, adding latency and token cost versus a single similarity lookup
Corpus changes require re-indexing (or incremental update machinery), so it's a poor fit for rapidly changing data

Heavy framework dependency: the notebook drives everything through the graphrag CLI and settings.yaml, giving less transparency and control than hand-built pipelines; debugging retrieval behavior is harder
Overkill for simple factoid lookup workloads where baseline top-k RAG is faster, cheaper, and accurate enough
Requires strong LLM (gpt-4o in the notebook) for extraction quality; weak models produce noisy graphs
how_it_works: The notebook installs the `graphrag` package and drives it entirely via CLI subcommands. Setup: credentials for Azure OpenAI or OpenAI are loaded from .env; `python -m graphrag.index --init --root data/graphrag` scaffolds a workspace, then the notebook rewrites settings.yaml (via PyYAML) to point llm at gpt-4o and embeddings at text-embedding-3-large. A Wikipedia article (Elon Musk) is scraped with requests/BeautifulSoup and dropped into the input folder. Indexing (`python -m graphrag.index --root ./data/graphrag`) then runs GraphRAG's pipeline: text chunking, LLM-based entity and relationship extraction, graph construction, Leiden community detection, and LLM summarization of each community — a lengthy, many-LLM-call, one-time build. Querying (`python -m graphrag.query`, wrapped in subprocess with GRAPHRAG_GLOBAL_SEARCH_MAX_TOKENS=10000 and a response_type instruction) supports two methods: 'local' search fans out from query-matched entities to their graph neighbors for entity-specific questions, and 'global' search map-reduces over community summaries for holistic corpus-level questions. A regex strips [Data: ...] provenance citations from responses. Though indexing dominates, the technique also spans retrieval (local/global search) and generation (map-reduce answer synthesis).
key_dependencies: subprocess (stdlib, to invoke the graphrag CLI)
key_code_signals: GRAPHRAG_GLOBAL_SEARCH_MAX_TOKENS env var to cap query context
re.sub(r'\[Data:.*?\]', '', text) to strip provenance tags
related_techniques: graph_rag (Graph RAG with LangChain): alternative hand-built knowledge-graph RAG implementation
graphrag_with_milvus_vectordb: alternative graph RAG storing relationship triplets in a vector DB, lighter-weight for multi-hop questions
graph_rag_local_attribution (Local Graph RAG with Verifiable Attribution): alternative graph RAG variant emphasizing verifiable citations
raptor: alternative for corpus-level/global questions via a recursive summary tree instead of an entity graph
hierarchical_indices: lighter-weight alternative for two-level summary-then-chunk retrieval
simple_rag: baseline chunk-embedding RAG that GraphRAG replaces when global understanding is needed
summary: Microsoft GraphRAG replaces flat chunk-embedding retrieval with an LLM-built knowledge graph over the corpus. At index time the graphrag CLI (python -m graphrag.index) chunks documents, uses GPT-4o to extract entities and relationships, builds a graph, detects communities with the Leiden algorithm, and writes LLM-generated summaries for each community; text-embedding-3-large embeddings are computed alongside. At query time (python -m graphrag.query) two modes exist: local search fans out from query-relevant entities to their graph neighbors for entity-specific questions, and global search map-reduces over community summaries to answer holistic whole-corpus sensemaking questions ('what are the major themes/accomplishments') that top-k similarity retrieval cannot answer because no single chunk contains the answer. It targets multi-hop questions and synthesis of disparate, scattered facts. Costs: indexing makes many LLM calls, so the build is slow and far more expensive than embedding-only indexing, and global queries add LLM map-reduce latency; corpus changes force re-indexing. The notebook configures settings.yaml for Azure OpenAI or plain OpenAI and demonstrates both search modes on a scraped Wikipedia article.
notes_for_chunking: Structure: 24 cells, markdown-heavy front, thin code. Cell 0 is a book-promo ad (strip). Cells 1 and 23 are view-tracker badge pixels, one as a 'raw' cell (strip; note raw cell type exists here). Cell 2 is one giant self-contained markdown cell holding the entire conceptual content (Overview, Motivation, Key Components, Method Details indexing/query stages, Benefits, Conclusion) under H1/H2 headers — ideal to split by H2 but the headers use inline formatting inconsistently (some 'headers' are plain bold lines like 'Indexing Stage'). Cell 3 is an image-only markdown cell (relative SVG path, strip or keep as figure ref). Cell 5 is a bare .env template code cell with no imports (easily mistaken for config junk). Three separate near-duplicate pip-install cells (6, 8, 10) — strip all. Real code is only 5 cells: client setup, web scrape, YAML config + CLI index, subprocess query helper, two query invocations. Code cells depend on prior cells (env vars, `oai` unused after setup, `elon` text) so they are not fully self-contained; keep the yaml-config and query-helper cells paired with their preceding markdown explanations. Outputs are trivial (Markdown display objects), no base64 blobs to strip.
